game_name: Wizardry II: The Knight of Diamonds

danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player's home base; rulers killed, city vulnerable

danger_signals.visual_form: Six-level dungeon structure requiring mandatory exploration
danger_signals.meaning: Increasing danger and difficulty as player descends; no safe shortcuts via elevators

danger_signals.visual_form: Recommended character level 13+ with 'baptism by fire' for underleveled parties
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental danger communicates through mechanical difficulty; monsters scale threat

safety_signals.visual_form: Ability to save game within dungeon (unlike first game)
safety_signals.meaning: Reduced pressure to complete dungeon in single session; managed risk

safety_signals.visual_form: Available character templates for new players
safety_signals.meaning: Accessibility signal reducing early-game lethality threat

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Invaders laying siege to the city
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: City under siege state serves as visual indicator of faction vulnerability
Siege of Llylgamyn; presence implicit through city's distressed state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: City of Llylgamyn under siege; governance structure collapsed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Quest chain initiated: recovery of Knight of Diamonds armor becomes survival imperative
Party must venture into dangerous dungeon to obtain artifact (staff of Gnilda) to repel invaders

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Knight of Diamonds armor split across six dungeon levels
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mandatory exploration of all six levels; no level-skipping possible; each level contains armor piece

environmental_storytelling.example: Unlike first scenario where half of levels were optional, all six levels must be explored and completed; level structure communicates mandatory progression
Each dungeon level contains one piece of Knight's armor; collection requirement forces complete traversal and signals multi-stage quest completion

environmental_storytelling.visual_manifestation: City under attack sets urgency context; player's motivation externalized through world state rather than abstract quest log

environmental_storytelling.example: Removal of elevators from level design communicates that party cannot retreat safely; increases perceived danger through navigation constraints

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Absence signals power vacuum and desperate need for player intervention
Implied threatening behavior outside dungeon; creates time pressure context for dungeon exploration
Expected to undertake high-risk dungeon delve; underleveled characters face punishing difficulty

notes: Wizardry II uses minimal explicit visual communication due to NES technical limitations. World-state is primarily conveyed through narrative framing (city under siege), mechanical design (mandatory level exploration, no elevators), and quest structure (artifact fragmentation across levels). Danger is communicated through difficulty scaling and mechanical challenge rather than explicit visual environmental cues. The game relies on player understanding from predecessor and text-based story setup rather than in-game visual manifestations of political/military situation. Safety signals are introduced through quality-of-life improvements (mid-dungeon saves, character generation) over the first game.
sources_used: wikipedia:Wizardry II: The Knight of Diamonds
